text: Duhigg's investigation of Febreze's failed product launch revealed a counterintuitive truth about rewards: they must satisfy a craving that already exists, or create a new one. Procter & Gamble's original campaign positioned Febreze as an odor eliminator for homes with serious smells. Sales were flat — people with chronically smelly environments had adapted to it and felt no craving for freshness. The turnaround came when marketers repositioned Febreze as a finishing ritual: a spritz at the end of cleaning that delivered a sensory signal of completion. The product created a new craving — the feeling of a job done — and the habit loop snapped into place almost immediately.
question: Why did Febreze's original marketing campaign fail, according to the passage?

options.text: Consumers did not understand how the odor elimination technology worked
The cue was too subtle to reliably trigger the cleaning routine
People with chronically smelly homes had adapted and felt no craving for freshness
The reward of freshness competed with an existing habit loop tied to cleaning products
